pitch_rationale: The homes display complex multi-plane hip roofs with moderate shadow lengths extending roughly 5–8 ft from the eave edges (per the 50-ft scale bar), consistent with a 6:12 pitch. The ridge lines are well-inset from the eave perimeter but the visible roof plane widths are broad, ruling out anything steeper than 8:12. The triangular hip-end gables show moderate protrusion from overhead, not the dramatic tapering seen on 9:12+ roofs. Regional context (Spring, TX — Houston suburb) strongly favors 6:12 as the standard pitch for upscale suburban hip-roofed homes in this warm climate with no snow-load requirements. The dark shingle texture shows subtle but not dramatic slope definition from the satellite vantage, further corroborating a moderate 6:12 pitch over steeper alternatives.
footprint_rationale: Target home identified as the large complex hip-roofed residence near the image center (coordinates 30.1117, -95.5091). Bounding box traced from approximately x=435–650 (215 px = 45.6 ft) by y=385–685 (300 px = 63.6 ft), yielding a bounding area of 2,895 sq ft. Applied a 78% fill factor to account for multiple wing cutouts, recesses, and the complex multi-section plan shape, arriving at ~2,258 sq ft footprint. Roof is a fully hipped multi-section design (no gable rakes). Ridge estimated from 4–5 short ridge lines atop each hip section (~190 px total). Hip lines counted at ~14 diagonal edges across all sections (~810 px). Valleys counted at ~5 inward junction lines (~265 px). Eaves traced around the complex lower perimeter (~410 px). Confidence is moderate (0.43) due to the high complexity of the roof geometry and difficulty precisely resolving individual sections at this resolution.
